commonIssues: Acid-loving: needs soil pH 4.0-5.5; chlorosis (yellowing leaves) appears in alkaline soil. Use ericaceous mix and avoid hard tap water; rainwater is preferred.
Shallow-rooted and sensitive to inconsistent moisture; needs about one inch (25 mm) of water per week and more during hot, dry spells, but dislikes waterlogging.
Partially self-fertile: a single plant will crop, but planting two or more cultivars together gives larger, earlier berries and bigger yields.

Container minimum (about 38 L / 10 gal) suits compact/half-high cultivars; full-size northern highbush bushes (mature 1.5-1.8 m / 5-6 ft tall and wide) need a large 57-75 L (15-20 gal) container or half barrel for long-term growth.
Slow to crop: little fruit in the first 2-3 years, with full yields after about 5 years.

source.citation: University of Minnesota Extension — Growing blueberries in the home garden (soil pH 4.0-5.5, full sun, space ~3 ft apart, plant two or more varieties for pollination, hardy to USDA zones 3-4, harvest ~July, acid/azalea fertilizer in spring, little fruit first 2-3 years).

Royal Horticultural Society — How to grow blueberries (ericaceous compost pH 4.5-5.5, container 30 cm dia for young / 45-50 cm dia for larger plants, keep compost slightly moist with rainwater not tap water, feed every 2-3 weeks Apr-Aug, sunny position, harvest Jun-Sep, most blueberries partially self-fertile so one plant crops but cross-pollination gives larger harvests, hardy in the UK).

Clemson University HGIC — Blueberry (water thoroughly two to three times per week during dry spells; azalea-type fertilizer 10-5-4 / 10-8-8 / 11-7-7 with sidedressing roughly every six weeks through the season; full sunlight; southern highbush generally self-fertile but larger yields with cross-pollination; harvest May-July; container dwarf cultivars).

Clemson University Plant Profiles — Vaccinium corymbosum 'Jubilee' / highbush blueberry (acid-loving, pH 4.5-5.5; mature plants ~5-6 ft tall and wide; full sun, part shade acceptable; 3 in mulch; needs at least one inch of water per week; shallow roots sensitive to inconsistent moisture).

University of Maryland Extension — Growing Blueberries in Containers (half-high cultivars do best in a 10-gallon (~38 L) or larger container; large northern highbush types need a half wine barrel or 15-20 gallon (~57-75 L) container).

Confidence note — heatStressC (32 C) is a conservative estimate: extension sources emphasize cold-hardiness and consistent moisture rather than a precise upper heat-stress threshold, so this value is approximate and intended as a watering/shade trigger, not a hard limit. hotWeatherMultiplier and rainSkipThresholdMm are derived operational defaults from the 'one inch (25 mm) per week, more in heat' guidance rather than literal cited numbers.
